questions.questionText: The peptide bond has partial double-bond character due to resonance between C=O and C-N. This has which structural consequence?
questions.optionA: Free rotation around the peptide bond
questions.optionB: PLANARITY of the peptide bond unit (6 atoms: Cα-C(=O)-N(-H)-Cα are coplanar), restricting rotation and limiting backbone conformations — this is why Ramachandran plots show only certain φ/ψ angle combinations are allowed
questions.optionD: All amino acids adopt the same conformation
questions.explanation: Peptide bond — structural implications: Resonance: electrons delocalized between C=O and C-N → partial double-bond character for C-N → PLANAR (trans configuration preferred, except Pro which can be cis ~6%). Ramachandran plot: plots backbone dihedral angles φ (phi, around N-Cα) and ψ (psi, around Cα-C). Only ~30% of φ/ψ combinations are sterically ALLOWED (no atomic clashes). α-helix: φ = -57°, ψ = -47°. β-sheet: φ = -120°, ψ = +120° (anti-parallel). Left-handed helix: only glycine (no side chain → no steric clash). Proline: φ locked at ~-60° (cyclic side chain), further restricts conformational freedom → helix breaker. Peptide bond hydrolysis: thermodynamically favorable but kinetically SLOW (t½ ~500 years at pH 7, 25°C) → requires proteases. Reference: Harper's — Peptide Bond.

questions.questionText: A patient with osteogenesis imperfecta has a glycine → serine substitution in the α1 chain of type I collagen. This is devastating because:
questions.optionB: Glycine (the smallest AA, R=H) must occupy EVERY THIRD position in the collagen triple helix (Gly-X-Y repeat) because only H fits in the crowded interior; ANY substitution at Gly position disrupts triple helix formation causing a dominant-negative effect
questions.explanation: Collagen Gly-X-Y — structural requirement: Collagen triple helix: 3 left-handed polyproline II helices wound into right-handed superhelix. INTERIOR is extremely TIGHT — only glycine (R=H) can fit at every third position. Any larger side chain → steric clash → triple helix disruption. Common OI mutations: Gly → Ser, Gly → Cys, Gly → Arg. DOMINANT-NEGATIVE: one mutant chain disrupts entire triple helix (3 chains involved) → worse than null mutation. 'Suicide model': mutant chains incorporate → disrupt folding from C-terminus to N-terminus → partially unfolded triple helix → susceptible to proteolysis. Severity gradient: mutations closer to C-terminus are MORE severe (folding proceeds C→N). OI types: I (mild, quantitative ↓), II (lethal), III (progressive deforming), IV (moderate). Reference: Harper's — OI Collagen.
questions.conceptTag: OI glycine substitution triple helix disruption

questions.questionText: Amino acids are classified as essential or non-essential. The concept of 'conditionally essential' amino acids is clinically relevant because:
questions.optionA: They are never needed in diet
questions.optionB: Tyrosine (from Phe), cysteine (from Met), arginine, glutamine, and proline become essential when their precursor supply is limited or demand exceeds synthesis capacity (e.g., premature neonates, critical illness, inborn errors)
questions.optionC: They are essential only in plants
questions.optionD: All amino acids are equally essential
questions.explanation: Conditionally essential amino acids: Normally NON-ESSENTIAL but become essential under specific conditions: (1) TYROSINE: synthesized from phenylalanine (PAH). In PKU: Tyr becomes essential (no PAH → can't convert Phe→Tyr). In premature infants: immature PAH. (2) CYSTEINE: from methionine via transsulfuration (CBS, cystathionase). In homocystinuria or premature infants: becomes essential. (3) ARGININE: synthesized via urea cycle, but in neonates (especially premature): urea cycle enzymes immature → insufficient synthesis. Critical in NEC (necrotizing enterocolitis) prevention. (4) GLUTAMINE: most abundant AA, fuel for enterocytes/immune cells. In critical illness/burns: demand exceeds synthesis → conditionally essential (ICU supplementation). (5) PROLINE: from glutamate. In wound healing states: demand increases. Essential AAs mnemonic: PVT TIM HaLL. Reference: Harper's — Conditionally Essential AA.

questions.questionText: Collagen is unique because it contains:
questions.optionB: Hydroxyproline and hydroxylysine (requires vitamin C for hydroxylation)
questions.explanation: Collagen: most abundant protein in the body. Unique features: rich in glycine (every 3rd residue — Gly-X-Y repeat), proline and hydroxyproline. Hydroxylation requires vitamin C (ascorbic acid) as cofactor for prolyl and lysyl hydroxylase. Vitamin C deficiency → scurvy (defective collagen → bleeding gums, poor wound healing, perifollicular hemorrhages). Triple helix structure (3 left-handed chains form right-handed superhelix). Type I (bone, skin) most abundant. Reference: Harper's — Collagen.

questions.questionText: Glycine is the only achiral amino acid (no D/L forms). Besides being the simplest AA, glycine has unique metabolic roles:
questions.optionB: Glycine is required for: heme synthesis (with succinyl-CoA), glutathione (Glu-Cys-Gly), purine ring (C4, C5, N7), creatine synthesis, conjugation reactions (bile acids, drugs), collagen (every 3rd residue), and inhibitory neurotransmission (glycinergic synapses in spinal cord)
questions.optionC: It is only used in collagen
questions.explanation: Glycine — metabolic versatility: Despite being simplest, glycine participates in more biosynthetic reactions than any other AA: (1) HEME: glycine + succinyl-CoA → ALA (ALAS, rate-limiting), (2) GLUTATHIONE: γ-Glu-Cys-GLY (antioxidant tripeptide), (3) PURINES: C4, C5, N7 of purine ring, (4) CREATINE: glycine + arginine → guanidinoacetate → creatine, (5) COLLAGEN: Gly-X-Y repeat (every 3rd position, ~33%), (6) CONJUGATION: glycine conjugation of bile acids (glycocholate) and drugs (hippuric acid from benzoate), (7) NEUROTRANSMITTER: inhibitory in spinal cord (strychnine = glycine receptor antagonist), (8) ONE-CARBON: glycine cleavage system → N5,N10-methylene-THF. Non-ketotic hyperglycinemia: glycine cleavage system defect → encephalopathy. Reference: Harper's — Glycine Metabolism.

questions.questionText: Hemoglobin is an example of which level of protein structure?
questions.optionB: Secondary
questions.explanation: Hemoglobin: quaternary structure — tetramer consisting of 2α and 2β globin subunits, each with a heme group (Fe2+ protoporphyrin IX). Exhibits cooperativity (sigmoid O2 dissociation curve) and allosteric regulation (2,3-DPG, CO2, H+, temperature). Myoglobin: single subunit → tertiary structure only → hyperbolic curve (no cooperativity). T-state (taut, deoxy): low affinity. R-state (relaxed, oxy): high affinity. Reference: Harper's — Hemoglobin.

questions.questionText: The isoelectric point (pI) of an amino acid is the pH at which:
questions.optionA: The amino acid is fully protonated
questions.optionB: The amino acid has zero net charge (zwitterion form predominates)
questions.optionC: The amino acid is fully deprotonated
questions.explanation: pI (isoelectric point): pH at which the amino acid or protein has zero net electrical charge and does not migrate in an electric field. For amino acids without ionizable side chains: pI = (pK1 + pK2)/2. For acidic amino acids: pI = (pK1 + pKR)/2. For basic amino acids: pI = (pK2 + pKR)/2. At pI, protein solubility is minimum → casein precipitation (milk curdling). Electrophoresis separates proteins based on charge at given pH. Reference: Harper's — Isoelectric Point.

questions.questionText: HbS (Glu6Val β-chain) confers malaria resistance in heterozygotes (sickle cell trait). The biochemical mechanism of this protection is:
questions.optionA: HbS kills the malaria parasite directly
questions.optionB: Parasitized RBCs with HbAS sickle at LOW O2 → sickling triggers premature splenic removal of infected cells AND reduces intraerythrocytic parasite growth due to HbS polymerization disrupting parasite feeding vacuole
questions.optionD: HbS increases immune response to malaria
questions.explanation: Sickle trait and malaria resistance (balanced polymorphism): HbAS heterozygotes (~40% HbS): (1) Normal O2: no sickling → no symptoms. (2) P. falciparum infection: parasite metabolism consumes O2 → LOCAL HYPOXIA within infected RBC → HbS polymerizes → sickling. Consequences: (a) Sickling marks cell for SPLENIC REMOVAL (before parasite matures to schizont stage), (b) HbS polymers DISRUPT parasite feeding vacuole → impaired hemoglobin digestion, (c) Increased oxidative stress in parasitized cells → parasite death, (d) Reduced cytoadherence (sickled cells express less PfEMP1 → less sequestration). This explains HbS gene frequency in malaria-endemic regions (Africa, Mediterranean, Middle East, India). G6PD deficiency, thalassemia also confer partial malaria resistance. Reference: Harper's — Sickle Trait Malaria.

questions.questionText: Disulfide bonds (Cys-S-S-Cys) stabilize protein tertiary/quaternary structure. Protein disulfide isomerase (PDI) in the ER ensures:
questions.optionB: CORRECT disulfide bond pairing — PDI catalyzes breakage and reformation of incorrect disulfide bonds until the thermodynamically most stable (native) pairing is achieved; it is essential for proper folding of secretory and membrane proteins
questions.optionD: Disulfide bonds form in the cytoplasm
questions.explanation: Protein disulfide isomerase (PDI): ER lumen is OXIDIZING (unlike reducing cytoplasm) → permits disulfide bond formation. Problem: random disulfide pairing among multiple Cys residues → many incorrect combinations. PDI: thioredoxin-family enzyme with CXXC motif → catalyzes: (1) OXIDATION: forms disulfide bonds (Cys-SH + SH-Cys → Cys-S-S-Cys), (2) REDUCTION: breaks incorrect bonds, (3) ISOMERIZATION: rearranges incorrect to correct pairings. PDI is recycled by Ero1 (ER oxidoreductin 1, uses O2). Example: insulin: 3 disulfide bonds (2 interchain, 1 intrachain) — correct pairing essential. Immunoglobulins: multiple disulfide bonds. PDI deficiency → protein misfolding → ER stress → unfolded protein response (UPR). Reference: Harper's — Disulfide Bond Formation.
questions.conceptTag: PDI disulfide isomerase ER protein folding

questions.questionText: Bradford and BCA assays are used to quantify total protein concentration. The principle underlying the Bradford assay is:
questions.optionB: Coomassie Brilliant Blue G-250 dye binds to hydrophobic regions and positively charged residues (Arg, Lys, His) of proteins → shifts dye absorption from 465nm (red/brown) to 595nm (blue) proportional to protein concentration
questions.explanation: Protein quantification methods: BRADFORD ASSAY: Coomassie blue G-250 binds proteins (hydrophobic + electrostatic interactions with Arg, Lys, His). Free dye: red/brown (λmax 465nm). Bound dye: BLUE (λmax 595nm). Advantages: fast (2 min), sensitive (1-20 μg). Disadvantages: variable response to different proteins (detergent interference). BCA ASSAY (bicinchoninic acid): proteins reduce Cu²⁺ → Cu⁺ (in alkaline, similar to biuret) → Cu⁺ chelated by BCA → purple complex (562nm). More uniform protein-to-protein response. LOWRY: Folin-Ciocalteu reagent + biuret reaction → blue (750nm). Sensitive but many interferences. UV 280nm: Trp, Tyr, Phe absorption — no reagents but less sensitive. BIURET: simplest, least sensitive → Cu²⁺ + peptide bonds (≥2) → violet complex (540nm). Reference: Harper's — Protein Assays.

questions.questionText: Proline is unique among amino acids because it:
questions.optionB: Contains an imino group (pyrrolidine ring) → introduces kinks/bends in protein chains
questions.explanation: Proline: imino acid (secondary amine — α-amino group is part of a rigid pyrrolidine ring). This restricts rotation of the polypeptide backbone → introduces kinks/bends, disrupts α-helices and β-sheets. Proline is an 'α-helix breaker.' Important in collagen (Gly-X-Y, where X is often proline, Y is often hydroxyproline). Trans-cis isomerization of proline peptide bonds is catalyzed by prolyl isomerases (cyclophilin — target of cyclosporine). Reference: Harper's — Proline.

questions.questionText: Denaturation of proteins involves loss of:
questions.optionB: Secondary, tertiary, and quaternary structure (but primary structure is preserved)
questions.explanation: Denaturation: disruption of non-covalent interactions (H-bonds, hydrophobic, ionic, van der Waals) and disulfide bonds → loss of secondary, tertiary, and quaternary structure → loss of biological activity. Primary structure (peptide bonds) is NOT broken. Caused by: heat, extreme pH, urea, guanidine HCl, organic solvents, heavy metals, detergents. Example: egg white coagulation (heat → albumin denaturation). Renaturation possible in some cases (Anfinsen experiment — RNase A). Reference: Harper's — Denaturation.

questions.questionText: The amino acid that absorbs UV light at 280 nm (used for protein quantification) is primarily:
questions.optionB: Tryptophan (and tyrosine to a lesser extent)
questions.explanation: Aromatic amino acids absorb UV: Tryptophan (~280 nm, strongest absorber), Tyrosine (~275 nm), Phenylalanine (~258 nm). Protein concentration can be estimated by measuring absorbance at 280 nm (mainly due to Trp and Tyr). All amino acids absorb at 205-210 nm (peptide bond). DNA/RNA absorb at 260 nm (purine/pyrimidine bases). A260/A280 ratio used to assess protein purity. Reference: Harper's — UV Absorption.

questions.questionText: Glutathione is a tripeptide composed of:
questions.optionB: γ-Glutamyl-Cysteinyl-Glycine
questions.explanation: Glutathione (GSH): γ-glutamyl-cysteinyl-glycine. The γ-peptide bond (between γ-COOH of glutamate and α-NH2 of cysteine) protects it from peptidase cleavage. Active -SH group of cysteine → antioxidant. GSH + H2O2 → GSSG (oxidized, via glutathione peroxidase, requires Se). GSSG → GSH (glutathione reductase, requires NADPH from HMP shunt). G6PD deficiency → low NADPH → low GSH → hemolytic anemia under oxidative stress. Reference: Harper's — Glutathione.

questions.questionText: Chaperones (heat shock proteins) assist in:
questions.optionB: Proper protein folding and preventing misfolding/aggregation
questions.explanation: Molecular chaperones (HSP70, HSP60/chaperonins, HSP90): assist newly synthesized polypeptides in achieving correct 3D folding, prevent aggregation under stress (heat, oxidative). They do NOT provide structural information — the folding code is in the amino acid sequence (Anfinsen's dogma). Chaperonin (GroEL/GroES in bacteria, HSP60/10 in eukaryotes): provides isolated chamber for protein folding. Misfolded proteins → proteasome (ubiquitin-tagged) or cause diseases (Alzheimer's, prion). Reference: Harper's — Chaperones.

questions.questionText: Albumin (68 kDa, pI 4.7) is the most abundant plasma protein. In nephrotic syndrome, the specific reason albumin is preferentially lost in urine is:
questions.optionB: The glomerular filtration barrier has both SIZE and CHARGE selectivity — albumin's relatively small size (68 kDa, near filtration cutoff) combined with LOSS OF NEGATIVE CHARGE on the GBM (heparan sulfate proteoglycan depletion) in nephrotic syndrome allows albumin passage
questions.optionC: Albumin is actively secreted into urine
questions.explanation: Albumin and glomerular filtration: Normal glomerular barrier: (1) SIZE barrier: podocyte slit diaphragm (~40nm pores) → excludes proteins >60 kDa, (2) CHARGE barrier: GBM rich in HEPARAN SULFATE PROTEOGLYCANS → negative charge → repels negatively charged proteins. Albumin: 68 kDa (borderline for size barrier) + pI 4.7 (NEGATIVELY charged at physiological pH) → DOUBLY protected. In nephrotic syndrome (minimal change, FSGS, membranous): loss of podocyte foot processes + depletion of GBM negative charges → both barriers compromised → massive albuminuria (>3.5g/day). Consequences: hypoalbuminemia → decreased oncotic pressure → EDEMA. Compensatory hepatic protein synthesis → hyperlipidemia (increased lipoprotein synthesis). Loss of anticoagulant proteins (AT-III) → hypercoagulability. Reference: Harper's — Albumin Nephrotic Syndrome.
questions.conceptTag: Albumin GBM charge size nephrotic syndrome

questions.questionText: The amino acid that is both glucogenic and ketogenic is:
questions.optionB: Isoleucine (and also tryptophan, phenylalanine, tyrosine, threonine)
questions.explanation: Classification: Purely ketogenic: leucine, lysine (only produce acetyl-CoA/acetoacetyl-CoA). Both glucogenic and ketogenic: isoleucine, tryptophan, phenylalanine, tyrosine, threonine (produce both glucogenic and ketogenic intermediates). All other amino acids are purely glucogenic (produce pyruvate, oxaloacetate, α-KG, succinyl-CoA, fumarate). Mnemonic for purely ketogenic: 'LL' (Leucine, Lysine). Reference: Harper's — Amino Acid Catabolism.

questions.questionText: HbA1c (glycated hemoglobin) is formed by:
questions.optionB: Non-enzymatic glycation of Hb with glucose (Amadori rearrangement)
questions.explanation: HbA1c: formed by non-enzymatic glycation (Maillard reaction) — glucose reacts with N-terminal valine of the β-chain via Schiff base → Amadori rearrangement → stable ketoamine (HbA1c). Reflects average blood glucose over preceding 2-3 months (RBC lifespan ~120 days). Normal: <5.7%. Prediabetes: 5.7-6.4%. Diabetes: ≥6.5%. Used for diagnosis and monitoring of diabetes. Falsely elevated: iron deficiency. Falsely low: hemolysis, transfusion. Reference: Harper's — HbA1c.

questions.questionText: Ehlers-Danlos syndrome is most commonly caused by defects in:
questions.optionB: Collagen (various types — synthesis, structure, or processing defects)
questions.explanation: Ehlers-Danlos syndrome: group of connective tissue disorders due to defects in collagen synthesis, structure, or processing. Features: hyperextensible skin, joint hypermobility, tissue fragility, easy bruising. Multiple types: Classic (COL5A), Vascular (COL3A1 — most severe, arterial rupture), Hypermobility, Kyphoscoliotic (lysyl hydroxylase deficiency). Marfan syndrome: fibrillin-1 (FBN1) defect. Osteogenesis imperfecta: Type I collagen (COL1A1/COL1A2). Reference: Harper's — Collagen Disorders.

questions.questionText: The Edman degradation is used to determine:
questions.optionB: Amino acid sequence from the N-terminal end (one residue at a time)
questions.explanation: Edman degradation: sequential removal and identification of amino acids from the N-terminal end using phenylisothiocyanate (PITC/Edman reagent). PITC reacts with free α-amino group → thiazolinone derivative → cleaved → identified by chromatography → repeat. Can sequence ~50-60 residues from N-terminal. Longer proteins: fragmented first (trypsin, cyanogen bromide), then fragments sequenced. Now largely replaced by mass spectrometry. Reference: Harper's — Protein Sequencing.

questions.questionText: Nitrogen balance studies reveal that a patient in negative nitrogen balance could be in any of the following states EXCEPT:
questions.optionB: Active growth phase in children (positive balance — N intake > N excretion due to net protein synthesis for growth)
questions.explanation: Nitrogen balance — clinical states: N balance = N intake (dietary protein ÷ 6.25) - N output (urinary urea N + fecal N + skin/sweat N). POSITIVE: N intake > output (net protein synthesis): growing children, pregnancy, recovery from illness, anabolic steroids, refeeding. NEGATIVE: N output > intake (net protein catabolism): starvation, sepsis, major surgery, burns, trauma, cancer cachexia, immobilization, corticosteroid therapy, uncontrolled diabetes. EQUILIBRIUM: healthy adults on adequate diet (N in = N out). Clinical uses: assess protein adequacy in ICU (target positive balance for recovery). 1g urinary N ≈ 6.25g protein catabolized. 24-hour urine urea nitrogen (UUN) + 4g (insensible losses) = total N output. Reference: Harper's — Nitrogen Balance.
questions.conceptTag: Nitrogen balance positive negative clinical states

questions.questionText: A young man with emphysema at an early age is suspected to have alpha1-antitrypsin deficiency. This primarily reflects a defect in which protein class and function?
questions.optionA: Structural collagen involved in tensile strength
questions.optionB: Plasma serine protease inhibitor protecting elastin from neutrophil elastase
questions.optionC: Contractile actin in alveolar smooth muscle
questions.optionD: Membrane transporter in chloride channel family
questions.explanation: Correct answer: B. Alpha1-antitrypsin is a serpin (serine protease inhibitor) synthesized in liver; it inhibits neutrophil elastase and protects elastin in lung parenchyma. Deficiency causes early panacinar emphysema and liver disease due to misfolded protein accumulation. A and C are unrelated primary mechanisms; D describes CFTR pathology.

questions.questionText: Which feature best distinguishes myoglobin from hemoglobin?
questions.optionA: Myoglobin has quaternary structure with cooperative oxygen binding
questions.optionB: Myoglobin is a monomer with hyperbolic oxygen dissociation curve
questions.optionC: Myoglobin binds 2,3-BPG strongly and shifts curve to right
questions.optionD: Myoglobin carries most oxygen in blood plasma
questions.explanation: Correct answer: B. Myoglobin is monomeric (single polypeptide + heme) with high oxygen affinity and hyperbolic binding curve. Hemoglobin is tetrameric, cooperative, and allosterically regulated by 2,3-BPG, H+, and CO2. Myoglobin primarily stores oxygen in muscle.

questions.questionText: Which amino acid is most likely to be enriched in keratin and contributes to hardness of nails/hair via cross-linking?
questions.optionB: Glycine
questions.explanation: Correct answer: A. Keratin is rich in cysteine; extensive disulfide cross-links (cystine) increase mechanical strength and hardness, especially in hair and nails. Glycine is more characteristic of collagen repeat abundance.

questions.questionText: Which is the MOST appropriate statement regarding elastin?
questions.optionA: It has Gly-X-Y triplet as dominant sequence like collagen
questions.optionB: It provides elasticity and uses lysyl oxidase-dependent cross-links
questions.optionC: Its hydroxylation of proline is the key determinant of recoil
questions.optionD: It is the main oxygen-carrying protein in muscle
questions.explanation: Correct answer: B. Elastin provides stretch-recoil properties in vessels, lungs, and skin; cross-linking involves lysyl oxidase (copper-dependent), forming desmosine/isodesmosine links. A and C are collagen-centric statements; D is myoglobin function.

questions.questionText: In serum protein electrophoresis at alkaline pH, albumin migrates fastest toward anode mainly because it:
questions.optionB: Has highest positive charge
questions.optionD: Is glycosylated more than all globulins
questions.explanation: Correct answer: C. At alkaline pH, albumin is strongly negatively charged and migrates fastest to anode. It does not have highest molecular weight; migration depends on charge-to-mass characteristics and buffer pH.

questions.questionText: A patient with poor wound healing and bleeding gums has normal dietary protein intake but vitamin C deficiency. The immediate biochemical defect is impaired:
questions.optionB: Hydroxylation of proline/lysine residues in procollagen
questions.explanation: Correct answer: B. Vitamin C is required for prolyl and lysyl hydroxylase reactions during collagen maturation in ER. Defective hydroxylation reduces collagen stability, causing scurvy features.

questions.questionText: Which statement about denaturation is CORRECT?
questions.optionA: It always breaks peptide bonds first
questions.optionB: It can abolish enzymatic activity without altering primary sequence
questions.optionC: It invariably causes complete irreversible hydrolysis
questions.explanation: Correct answer: B. Denaturation disrupts higher-order folding (secondary/tertiary/quaternary), commonly abolishing biological function while primary peptide sequence remains intact. Peptide bond hydrolysis requires stronger chemical/enzymatic conditions.

questions.questionText: The buffering property of histidine in proteins is mainly due to which side-chain group?
questions.optionB: Guanidinium group
questions.explanation: Correct answer: C. Histidine contains an imidazole ring with pKa near physiological range, making it an effective proton donor/acceptor and important in enzyme active sites and hemoglobin buffering.

questions.questionText: Ninhydrin reacts with amino acids to produce a purple/blue color (Ruhemann's purple). The exception is proline, which gives:
questions.optionB: A YELLOW color — because proline is an IMINO acid (secondary amine, α-amino group is part of pyrrolidine ring), which reacts differently with ninhydrin to produce a yellow derivative instead of Ruhemann's purple
questions.explanation: Ninhydrin reaction — biochemical basis: Ninhydrin (triketohydrindene hydrate): reacts with α-amino acids in a multi-step reaction: (1) Oxidative deamination of AA → aldehyde + NH3 + CO2, (2) Released NH3 reacts with 2 molecules of ninhydrin → RUHEMANN'S PURPLE (diketohydrindylidene-diketohydrindamine, λmax 570nm). PROLINE exception: secondary amine (N is part of 5-membered pyrrolidine ring) → cannot undergo standard ninhydrin reaction → instead forms a YELLOW product (λmax 440nm). Hydroxyproline: same yellow reaction. Clinical/lab uses: (1) Amino acid detection in chromatography (TLC, paper), (2) Quantification of amino acids (amino acid analyzer), (3) Forensic: fingerprint detection (latent prints contain AA from sweat → ninhydrin spray → purple fingerprints). (4) Urinary amino acid screening (aminoaciduria). Reference: Harper's — Ninhydrin.

questions.questionText: A mutation replacing glycine in collagen triple helix is particularly disruptive because glycine:
questions.optionA: Is acidic and forms ionic bonds
questions.optionB: Is the only residue small enough to fit in helix core every third position
questions.explanation: Correct answer: B. Collagen requires glycine at every third residue for tight packing of triple helix core. Substitution disrupts helix and can cause disorders such as osteogenesis imperfecta (with COL1 defects).

questions.questionText: The most appropriate interpretation of low plasma albumin with pedal edema in chronic liver disease is:
questions.optionB: Reduced hepatic albumin synthesis causing decreased oncotic pressure
questions.explanation: Correct answer: B. Liver synthesizes albumin; chronic liver dysfunction lowers albumin, decreases plasma colloid osmotic pressure, and promotes fluid shift into interstitium causing edema/ascites.

questions.questionText: Which pair is correctly matched regarding structural proteins?
questions.optionA: Collagen - rich in hydroxyproline and tensile strength
questions.optionB: Elastin - predominant Gly-X-Y repeat with triple helix
questions.explanation: Correct answer: A. Collagen is rich in glycine/proline/hydroxyproline and provides tensile strength. Elastin provides recoil and does not have classical collagen triple-helix architecture. Keratin is an intracellular/intermediate filament protein, not proteoglycan.

questions.questionText: Prion diseases (transmissible spongiform encephalopathies) are caused by:
questions.optionB: Conformational change of normal PrPC protein to misfolded PrPSc (β-sheet rich, protease-resistant form)
questions.explanation: Prion diseases: infectious protein (no nucleic acid). Normal PrPC (predominantly α-helical, protease-sensitive) → misfolds to PrPSc (predominantly β-sheet, protease-resistant). PrPSc acts as a template → converts more PrPC to PrPSc → aggregation → spongiform degeneration of brain. Examples: CJD (Creutzfeldt-Jakob), kuru, BSE (mad cow), fatal familial insomnia, Gerstmann-Sträussler-Scheinker. Diagnosis: 14-3-3 protein in CSF, RT-QuIC assay. No treatment. Reference: Harper's — Prion Protein Misfolding.

questions.questionText: In amyloidosis, the characteristic staining pattern is:
questions.optionB: Congo red positive with apple-green birefringence under polarized light
questions.explanation: Amyloid: misfolded proteins deposited extracellularly as insoluble fibrils rich in β-pleated sheet structure. Staining: Congo red → salmon pink under light microscopy → APPLE-GREEN BIREFRINGENCE under polarized light (pathognomonic). Types: AL amyloid (immunoglobulin light chains — multiple myeloma), AA amyloid (serum amyloid A — chronic inflammation), Aβ (Alzheimer's), ATTR (transthyretin — familial/senile). Organs: kidney (nephrotic syndrome), heart, liver, tongue. Reference: Harper's — Amyloid Structure.

questions.questionText: Type IV collagen is found exclusively in basement membranes. Unlike fibrillar collagens (I, II, III), type IV collagen:
questions.optionB: Does NOT form fibrils; instead it forms a mesh-like NETWORK (sheet/lattice) because it retains its globular NC1 domains and contains interruptions in the Gly-X-Y repeat, enabling lateral associations that create the scaffold of basement membranes
questions.optionC: Is not a collagen at all
questions.explanation: Collagen types — fibrillar vs network: FIBRILLAR (I, II, III, V, XI): processed into tropocollagen → assemble into quarter-staggered fibrils → fibers (providing tensile strength). Type I: bone, tendon, skin (most abundant). Type II: cartilage, vitreous. Type III: blood vessels, skin (reticular). NETWORK (Type IV): (1) NOT processed — retains NC1 C-terminal and 7S N-terminal globular domains, (2) Has ~22 interruptions in Gly-X-Y repeat → flexibility, (3) NC1 domains of different molecules interact → head-to-head dimers, (4) 7S domains interact → tetramers, (5) Lateral associations → chicken-wire network → scaffold of ALL basement membranes. Goodpasture syndrome: autoantibodies against NC1 domain of α3 chain of type IV collagen (α3(IV)NC1) → GBM and alveolar BM damage → glomerulonephritis + pulmonary hemorrhage. Alport syndrome: mutations in α3/α4/α5(IV) chains. Reference: Harper's — Type IV Collagen.
questions.conceptTag: Type IV collagen network basement membrane Goodpasture

questions.questionText: SDS-PAGE separates proteins based on:
questions.optionB: Molecular weight (SDS denatures and coats all proteins with uniform negative charge)
questions.explanation: SDS-PAGE: SDS (sodium dodecyl sulfate) denatures proteins and coats them with uniform negative charge proportional to length → migration depends solely on molecular weight (smaller proteins migrate faster). β-mercaptoethanol or DTT reduces disulfide bonds. Unlike native PAGE (which separates by charge and size), SDS-PAGE separates by size only. Western blot: SDS-PAGE followed by transfer to membrane → probed with specific antibody → detects specific proteins. 2D gel: first dimension = IEF (charge), second = SDS-PAGE (size). Reference: Harper's — Protein Electrophoresis.

questions.questionText: Osteogenesis imperfecta is most commonly caused by mutations in genes encoding:
questions.optionB: Type I collagen (COL1A1 or COL1A2 genes)
questions.explanation: Osteogenesis imperfecta (brittle bone disease): defective Type I collagen (quantitative or qualitative defect in COL1A1/COL1A2). Features: fragile bones with recurrent fractures, blue sclerae (thin sclera → choroidal veins visible), hearing loss (otosclerosis), dentinogenesis imperfecta. Type I: mild, AD, reduced amount of normal collagen (null allele). Type II: lethal perinatal, AD (new mutation). Type III: severe, progressive deformity. Type IV: moderate. Marfan syndrome: fibrillin-1 (FBN1) defect. Reference: Harper's — OI.

questions.questionText: At the isoelectric point (pI), a protein has specific properties that are exploited in separation techniques. At pI:
questions.optionB: The protein has zero net charge → MINIMUM solubility (maximum precipitation), no electrophoretic migration, and maximum crystallization tendency — this is why isoelectric precipitation is used for casein isolation (pI 4.6) and immunoglobulin purification
questions.explanation: Isoelectric point — practical applications: At pI: net charge = 0 → no charge-charge repulsion between protein molecules → MINIMUM SOLUBILITY (maximum protein-protein interaction → aggregation/precipitation). Applications: (1) Isoelectric precipitation: add acid to milk → pH reaches casein pI (4.6) → casein precipitates (cheese making). (2) Salting out at pI: immunoglobulin purification — IgG pI ~7.5, adjust pH + ammonium sulfate → selective precipitation. (3) IEF (isoelectric focusing): separate proteins by pI in pH gradient. (4) 2D-PAGE: first dimension IEF (pI), second dimension SDS-PAGE (MW). pI calculation (simple amino acid): pI = (pKa1 + pKa2)/2 (for neutral AA). For acidic AA: average two lowest pKas. For basic AA: average two highest pKas. Reference: Harper's — Isoelectric Point Applications.

questions.questionText: Ehlers-Danlos syndrome (vascular type IV) is caused by defective:
questions.optionB: Type III collagen
questions.explanation: Ehlers-Danlos syndrome (EDS): group of connective tissue disorders. Vascular type (Type IV): COL3A1 mutation → defective Type III collagen → most dangerous — arterial/intestinal/uterine rupture, easy bruising, thin translucent skin. Classical type (I/II): COL5A mutation → joint hypermobility, skin hyperextensibility, poor wound healing (cigarette-paper scars). Kyphoscoliotic type: lysyl hydroxylase deficiency → similar + kyphoscoliosis, ocular fragility. Molecular type (VII): defective procollagen peptidase → severe joint hypermobility. Reference: Harper's — EDS Types.

questions.questionText: Scurvy (vitamin C deficiency) causes collagen dysfunction. The specific hydroxylation reaction that fails is:
questions.optionB: Prolyl 4-hydroxylase (Fe²⁺, O₂, ascorbate, α-KG) fails to convert proline → 4-hydroxyproline; unhydroxylated collagen cannot form stable triple helix at body temperature because hydroxyproline is critical for interchain hydrogen bonds via its OH group bridging to water molecules
questions.explanation: Vitamin C → collagen hydroxylation → scurvy: Prolyl 4-hydroxylase: Fe²⁺/α-ketoglutarate dioxygenase. Reaction: proline + O₂ + α-KG → 4-hydroxyproline + CO₂ + succinate. Fe²⁺ cycles: Fe²⁺ → Fe³⁺ → Fe²⁺ (ascorbate REDUCES Fe³⁺ back to Fe²⁺ — keeps enzyme active). Without ascorbate: Fe²⁺ → Fe³⁺ (inactive, cannot be recycled). Role of Hyp: 4-hydroxyproline-OH forms INTERCHAIN hydrogen bonds via WATER BRIDGES (Hyp-OH---H₂O---C=O of adjacent chain) → stabilizes triple helix. Hyp content determines Tm (melting temperature) of collagen: normal collagen Tm ~42°C (above body temp 37°C → stable). Unhydroxylated collagen Tm ~24°C → UNSTABLE at 37°C → denatures → weak collagen. Scurvy manifestations: bleeding gums, petechiae, poor wound healing, corkscrew hairs, subperiosteal hemorrhage, tooth loss. Reference: Harper's — Vitamin C Collagen.
questions.conceptTag: Prolyl hydroxylase ascorbate hydroxyproline triple helix

questions.questionText: Marfan syndrome is caused by a mutation in:
questions.optionB: Fibrillin-1 (FBN1 gene, chromosome 15)
questions.explanation: Marfan syndrome: autosomal dominant, FBN1 gene mutation → defective fibrillin-1 → abnormal microfibrils (scaffolding for elastic fibers). Also leads to increased TGF-β signaling. Features: tall stature, arachnodactyly (long spider-like fingers), pectus deformity, ectopia lentis (upward lens subluxation — unlike Homocystinuria: downward), mitral valve prolapse, aortic root dilation → dissection (main cause of death). Abraham Lincoln suspected to have had Marfan. Reference: Harper's — Fibrillin and Marfan.

questions.questionText: Western blot technique is used for detection of:
questions.optionB: Specific proteins (separated by SDS-PAGE, transferred to membrane, detected by antibody)
questions.explanation: Blotting techniques: Southern blot: DNA detection (named after Edwin Southern). Northern blot: RNA detection (named by analogy). Western blot: protein detection — proteins separated by SDS-PAGE → transferred to nitrocellulose/PVDF membrane → probed with primary antibody (specific) → secondary antibody (enzyme-conjugated) → detection. Used clinically as confirmatory test for HIV (detects anti-HIV antibodies against gp41, gp120, p24, etc.). ELISA is screening test for HIV. Reference: Harper's — Blotting Techniques.

questions.questionText: Hemoglobin displays cooperative oxygen binding (sigmoidal curve). The molecular mechanism underlying cooperativity is:
questions.optionA: Independent O₂ binding to each subunit
questions.optionB: T-state to R-state conformational transition: O₂ binding to one subunit induces conformational changes (breaking intersubunit salt bridges) transmitted to other subunits via the α1β2 interface → progressively increasing O₂ affinity (Hill coefficient ~2.8)
questions.optionD: Each subunit has different O₂ affinity that doesn't change
questions.explanation: Hemoglobin cooperativity — molecular mechanism: T-state (tense, deoxy): low O₂ affinity. Stabilized by: (1) Salt bridges between subunits (His146-Asp94, Lys40-His146), (2) 2,3-BPG binding in central cavity (β-subunit contacts), (3) His F8-Fe-heme geometry: Fe²⁺ slightly out of porphyrin plane. O₂ binds first subunit → Fe²⁺ pulled INTO porphyrin plane → His F8 moves → conformational change transmitted across α1β2 SLIDING INTERFACE → salt bridges break progressively → T→R transition. R-state (relaxed, oxy): HIGH O₂ affinity. Central cavity narrowed → 2,3-BPG expelled. Cooperativity: binding of each O₂ increases affinity for next → SIGMOIDAL curve (Hill equation: n = 2.8). Myoglobin: single subunit → HYPERBOLIC curve (n = 1, no cooperativity). Bohr effect: H⁺/CO₂ stabilize T-state → right-shift → O₂ unloading at tissues. Reference: Harper's — Cooperativity.
questions.conceptTag: Cooperativity T-R state transition Hill coefficient

questions.questionText: Ubiquitin-mediated proteasomal degradation of proteins involves:
questions.optionB: Tagging of target protein with polyubiquitin chains → recognition and degradation by 26S proteasome
questions.explanation: Ubiquitin-proteasome pathway: primary mechanism for regulated intracellular protein degradation. Steps: (1) E1 (ubiquitin-activating) activates ubiquitin with ATP, (2) E2 (ubiquitin-conjugating) transfers ubiquitin, (3) E3 (ubiquitin ligase — provides substrate specificity) attaches ubiquitin to target protein's lysine residue. Polyubiquitination (≥4 ubiquitins) → recognized by 26S proteasome → degraded to peptides. Regulates: cell cycle proteins (cyclins), transcription factors, misfolded proteins. Bortezomib: proteasome inhibitor (used in multiple myeloma). Reference: Harper's — Ubiquitin-Proteasome.

questions.questionText: A researcher performs Anfinsen's ribonuclease experiment: denaturation with urea + β-mercaptoethanol, then removal of denaturants. The protein REFOLDS correctly. This proves:
questions.optionB: ALL information for the three-dimensional structure of a protein is encoded in its PRIMARY amino acid sequence (Anfinsen's thermodynamic hypothesis) — the native fold represents the global free energy minimum
questions.explanation: Anfinsen's experiment (Nobel Prize, 1972): RNase A: 124 AA, 4 disulfide bonds (8 Cys → 105 possible pairings, only 1 is correct native state). Experiment: (1) Denature with 8M urea (breaks non-covalent bonds) + β-ME (reduces disulfide bonds) → fully unfolded, inactive. (2) Remove denaturants → protein SPONTANEOUSLY refolds → regains 100% activity with CORRECT disulfide bonds. Conclusion: ANFINSEN'S DOGMA — primary sequence contains all information for 3D structure; native fold is thermodynamic MINIMUM (most stable conformation). Levinthal's paradox: random sampling of all conformations would take longer than universe age → folding follows PATHWAYS (funnel model), not random search. In vivo: chaperones assist by preventing aggregation but do NOT provide folding information. Reference: Harper's — Anfinsen's Experiment.

questions.questionText: Sickle cell disease (HbS) is caused by substitution of:
questions.optionA: Valine for glutamic acid at position 6 of the α-chain
questions.optionB: Valine for glutamic acid at position 6 of the β-chain (Glu→Val, GAG→GTG)
questions.optionC: Lysine for glutamic acid at position 26 of the β-chain
questions.optionD: Histidine for glutamic acid at position 6 of the β-chain
questions.explanation: HbS: single point mutation in β-globin gene (chromosome 11), codon 6: GAG (glutamic acid, polar) → GTG (valine, non-polar/hydrophobic). This creates a hydrophobic patch on deoxy-HbS → polymerization under low O2 → sickling of RBCs. Heterozygotes (HbAS): sickle cell TRAIT — generally asymptomatic, provides relative protection against Plasmodium falciparum malaria. Homozygotes (HbSS): sickle cell DISEASE — vaso-occlusive crises, hemolytic anemia, splenic sequestration/autosplenectomy, acute chest syndrome. HbC: Glu→Lys at β6. Reference: Harper's — Hemoglobin Variants.

questions.questionText: A patient with tall stature, long limbs, and downward lens subluxation (ectopia lentis) with elevated homocysteine levels most likely has:
questions.optionB: Homocystinuria (cystathionine β-synthase deficiency)
questions.explanation: Homocystinuria vs Marfan comparison (classic NEET PG question): Both present with tall stature and marfanoid habitus. Key differences: Homocystinuria — lens subluxation DOWNWARD (inferonasal), intellectual disability, thromboembolism (leading cause of death), osteoporosis, AR inheritance, cystathionine β-synthase deficiency. Marfan — lens subluxation UPWARD (superotemporal), normal intelligence, aortic dissection (leading cause of death), AD inheritance, FBN1 mutation. Homocysteine level elevated in homocystinuria. Some cases respond to pyridoxine (B6). Reference: Harper's — Homocystinuria vs Marfan.

questions.questionText: Gamma-carboxylation of glutamic acid residues in clotting factors is a post-translational modification that requires:
questions.optionB: Vitamin K (required by γ-glutamyl carboxylase to activate factors II, VII, IX, X and proteins C, S)
questions.explanation: Vitamin K-dependent γ-carboxylation: γ-glutamyl carboxylase adds a second carboxyl group to specific glutamic acid residues → γ-carboxyglutamic acid (Gla) → enables calcium binding → essential for coagulation factor function. Vitamin K-dependent factors: II (prothrombin), VII, IX, X and anticoagulant proteins C, S, Z. Warfarin inhibits vitamin K epoxide reductase (VKOR) → blocks recycling of reduced vitamin K → inhibits γ-carboxylation → anticoagulant effect. Osteocalcin (bone) is also vitamin K-dependent. Reference: Harper's — Vitamin K Carboxylation.

questions.questionText: Prion diseases (CJD, BSE, kuru) are unique infectious agents because the prion protein (PrPSc) causes disease by:
questions.optionA: Introducing foreign DNA into the host genome
questions.optionB: Converting normal cellular PrPC (α-helix rich) into misfolded PrPSc (β-sheet rich) through conformational change — a protein-only infectious mechanism without nucleic acid
questions.explanation: Prion diseases — protein misfolding: PrPC (cellular prion protein): normal GPI-anchored glycoprotein on neurons, predominantly α-helical (42% α-helix, 3% β-sheet). Function uncertain but may involve copper binding, cell signaling. PrPSc (scrapie form): MISFOLDED conformer — predominantly β-SHEET (43% β-sheet, 30% α-helix). PrPSc is: (1) Resistant to proteases (partially), (2) Insoluble (aggregates as amyloid fibrils), (3) Resistant to standard sterilization. Mechanism: PrPSc acts as TEMPLATE — binds normal PrPC → induces conformational change to PrPSc → exponential propagation (no nucleic acid needed — Stanley Prusiner, Nobel 1997). Spongiform encephalopathy: neuronal vacuolation, astrocyte gliosis, amyloid plaques. Human: CJD (sporadic, familial, iatrogenic, variant), kuru, FFI, GSS. Reference: Harper's — Prion Diseases.

questions.questionText: Amyloidosis involves deposition of proteins in β-pleated sheet configuration. The amyloid protein deposited in Alzheimer's disease is:
questions.optionB: Amyloid β-peptide (Aβ42) — derived from abnormal processing of amyloid precursor protein (APP) by β-secretase and γ-secretase
questions.explanation: Amyloid proteins — disease specific deposits: Alzheimer's: Aβ42 (amyloid beta, 42 amino acids). APP (amyloid precursor protein, chromosome 21 — hence Down syndrome patients get early Alzheimer's) processed by β-secretase + γ-secretase → Aβ42 (amyloidogenic pathway). Normal: α-secretase cleaves within Aβ domain → no amyloid (non-amyloidogenic). Type 2 DM: IAPP (islet amyloid polypeptide/amylin) — deposited in pancreatic islets. AL amyloidosis: immunoglobulin LIGHT chains (multiple myeloma/plasma cell dyscrasias). AA amyloidosis: SAA (serum amyloid A — acute phase protein, chronic inflammation like RA, TB, FMF). Familial/senile: transthyretin (TTR) — cardiac amyloidosis. Dialysis: β2-microglobulin. Common feature: ALL form cross-β-sheet fibrils → Congo red staining → APPLE-GREEN birefringence under polarized light. Reference: Harper's — Amyloidosis.

questions.questionText: HbS (sickle hemoglobin) results from substitution of glutamic acid by valine at position 6 of the β-globin chain. This causes sickling because:
questions.optionA: Valine has a different charge than glutamic acid only
questions.optionB: Valine (hydrophobic) creates a sticky hydrophobic patch on the surface of deoxy-HbS that fits into a hydrophobic pocket on adjacent HbS molecules → polymerization into rigid fibers → distortion of RBC shape
questions.explanation: HbS polymerization — molecular mechanism: Glu6Val (GAG→GTG): Glutamic acid (charged, hydrophilic) → Valine (uncharged, HYDROPHOBIC). In DEOXY-HbS: β6 valine creates a hydrophobic protrusion on molecular surface → fits into a complementary hydrophobic pocket (formed by Phe85 and Leu88) on an ADJACENT deoxy-HbS molecule → intermolecular contact → polymerization into long rigid fibers (14-stranded helical polymer). Key: (1) Only DEOXY-HbS polymerizes (oxy-HbS has different conformation → pocket not accessible), (2) Polymerization is concentration-dependent (heterozygotes have ~40% HbS → don't sickle under normal conditions), (3) HbF (α2γ2) INHIBITS polymer formation (γ-chain cannot participate in sickle contacts), which is why hydroxyurea (↑HbF) is therapeutic. R-state (oxy): soluble. T-state (deoxy): polymerizes. Reference: Harper's — HbS Polymerization.

questions.questionText: Molecular chaperones (e.g., Hsp70, Hsp60/chaperonins) assist protein folding by:
questions.optionA: Providing the energy for peptide bond formation
questions.optionB: Binding to exposed hydrophobic surfaces of nascent/misfolded polypeptides → preventing aggregation and providing a protected environment for correct folding (using ATP hydrolysis)
questions.explanation: Molecular chaperones — protein quality control: Hsp70 (heat shock protein 70): binds EXPOSED HYDROPHOBIC patches on nascent polypeptides emerging from ribosome → prevents premature aggregation. ATP-dependent: ATP binding → lid opens → substrate binds, ATP hydrolysis → lid closes → substrate trapped. Co-chaperones: Hsp40 (stimulates Hsp70 ATPase), NEFs (nucleotide exchange factors). Hsp60/GroEL (chaperonins): barrel-shaped complex (GroEL + GroES in bacteria). Misfolded protein enters barrel → GroES caps → provides isolated hydrophilic chamber for folding → ATP hydrolysis → correctly folded protein released. Hsp90: assists later-stage folding of signaling proteins (steroid receptors, kinases). If folding fails despite chaperone attempts → ubiquitin-proteasome pathway degrades misfolded protein. Chaperone dysfunction → protein aggregation diseases (Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, Huntington's). Reference: Harper's — Molecular Chaperones.

questions.questionText: N-linked glycosylation of proteins begins in the rough ER with transfer of a preformed oligosaccharide from dolichol phosphate to:
questions.optionB: ASPARAGINE residues in the consensus sequence Asn-X-Ser/Thr (where X is any amino acid except proline)
questions.explanation: N-linked vs O-linked glycosylation: N-LINKED: en bloc transfer of Glc3Man9GlcNAc2 from dolichol-PP to ASPARAGINE (amide nitrogen) in sequon Asn-X-Ser/Thr. Occurs in ER (co-translational/early post-translational). Oligosaccharyltransferase (OST) catalyzes transfer. Subsequent processing in ER (glucosidases trim glucose, calnexin/calreticulin quality control) and Golgi (trimming mannoses, adding GlcNAc, galactose, sialic acid, fucose). Types: high-mannose, complex, hybrid. O-LINKED: post-translational in Golgi. Sugars added one at a time to SERINE/THREONINE (hydroxyl oxygen). Common: GalNAc (mucin-type). No consensus sequence. I-cell disease (mucolipidosis II): defective GlcNAc-1-phosphotransferase → cannot add mannose-6-phosphate tag → lysosomal enzymes secreted instead of targeted to lysosomes. Reference: Harper's — Glycosylation.

questions.questionText: Isoelectric focusing (IEF) separates proteins based on their:
questions.optionB: Isoelectric point (pI) — proteins migrate in a pH gradient until they reach the pH where their net charge is zero and they stop moving
questions.explanation: Protein separation techniques comparison: Isoelectric focusing (IEF): proteins loaded on gel with immobilized pH gradient. Electric field applied → protein migrates until it reaches pH = pI (net charge = 0) → stops. Resolution: very high (can separate proteins differing by 0.01 pI). Used in: 2D-PAGE (first dimension IEF by pI, second dimension SDS-PAGE by MW), hemoglobin variant analysis. SDS-PAGE: separates by molecular WEIGHT (SDS denatures and provides uniform negative charge). Western blot: SDS-PAGE + antibody detection (specific protein identification). 2D-PAGE: resolves thousands of proteins simultaneously. Mass spectrometry: identifies proteins by peptide mass fingerprinting after trypsin digestion. ELISA: antigen-antibody quantification. Reference: Harper's — Protein Electrophoresis.

questions.questionText: The ubiquitin-proteasome pathway degrades intracellular proteins. The signal for degradation includes:
questions.optionB: Polyubiquitination (attachment of a chain of ≥4 ubiquitin molecules via K48 linkage) which is recognized by the 26S proteasome for ATP-dependent proteolysis
questions.explanation: Ubiquitin-proteasome system (UPS): Major pathway for selective intracellular protein degradation: (1) UBIQUITIN TAGGING: E1 (ubiquitin-activating, ATP-dependent) → E2 (conjugating) → E3 (LIGASE, provides specificity — recognizes target protein). K48-linked polyubiquitin chain (≥4 ubiquitin molecules) = DEGRADATION signal. K63-linked = other functions (signaling, endocytosis, DNA repair, NOT degradation). (2) PROTEASOME: 26S complex (20S core with catalytic activity + two 19S regulatory caps). 19S: recognizes polyubiquitin, unfolds protein, deubiquitinates. 20S: barrel-shaped, 3 catalytic activities (chymotrypsin-like, trypsin-like, caspase-like) → protein → peptides (8-10 AA). Functions: cell cycle control (cyclin degradation), transcription factor regulation (NF-κB), MHC class I antigen presentation, removal of misfolded proteins. Bortezomib: proteasome inhibitor — treats multiple myeloma. Reference: Harper's — Ubiquitin-Proteasome.

questions.questionText: Collagen cross-linking requires the enzyme lysyl oxidase, which needs copper as a cofactor. Defective cross-linking results in:
questions.optionB: Fragile connective tissue with hyperextensible skin and hypermobile joints (as seen in Ehlers-Danlos syndrome type VI and copper deficiency/Menkes disease)
questions.explanation: Collagen cross-linking — clinical significance: LYSYL OXIDASE: copper-dependent enzyme that oxidatively deaminates lysine/hydroxylysine residues in tropocollagen → reactive aldehydes (allysine) → spontaneous cross-linking between adjacent collagen molecules → mature, strong collagen fibers. Deficient cross-linking: (1) Menkes disease: copper deficiency (ATP7A mutation) → lysyl oxidase inactive → weak collagen + elastin → tortuous blood vessels, lax skin, bladder diverticula. (2) Ehlers-Danlos type VI (kyphoscoliotic): lysyl hydroxylase deficiency → decreased hydroxylysine → impaired cross-linking + impaired N-linked glycosylation of collagen. (3) Lathyrism: β-aminopropionitrile (from Lathyrus sativus) → INHIBITS lysyl oxidase directly → aortic aneurysms, skeletal abnormalities. (4) D-penicillamine: chelates copper → can impair lysyl oxidase (side effect). Reference: Harper's — Collagen Cross-linking.

questions.questionText: Hemoglobin electrophoresis at alkaline pH shows migration order (fastest to slowest cathode to anode):
questions.optionB: HbA (fastest toward anode), then HbF, then HbS, then HbC (slowest) — mnemonic: 'A Fast Stallion Can't'
questions.explanation: Hemoglobin electrophoresis — alkaline (cellulose acetate, pH 8.6): Migration toward ANODE (positive): fastest → slowest: A > F > S > C. Mnemonic: 'A Fast Stallion Can't' or 'Afghanistan.' Basis: net charge at alkaline pH. HbA: Glu6 (negative charge) → migrates fast. HbS: Val6 (neutral, lost negative charge) → migrates slower. HbC: Lys6 (positive, gained positive charge) → migrates slowest. Problem: HbS co-migrates with HbD, HbG. HbC co-migrates with HbE, HbO. Resolution: ACID (citrate agar) electrophoresis at pH 6.2 separates HbS from HbD and HbC from HbE. HPLC and capillary electrophoresis: modern methods that quantify hemoglobin fractions precisely. IEF: highest resolution for Hb variants. Reference: Harper's — Hemoglobin Electrophoresis.

questions.questionText: Protein denaturation disrupts secondary, tertiary, and quaternary structures while keeping the PRIMARY structure (peptide bonds) intact. The most effective denaturing agent is:
questions.optionB: 6M guanidine hydrochloride or 8M urea — these disrupt hydrogen bonds and hydrophobic interactions, completely unfolding proteins
questions.explanation: Protein denaturation — agents and mechanisms: CHEMICAL: (1) Guanidine HCl (6M) and urea (8M): most potent — disrupt ALL non-covalent interactions (H-bonds, hydrophobic, van der Waals, ionic). Guanidinium ions form H-bonds with peptide backbone, destabilize hydrophobic core. (2) SDS: disrupts hydrophobic interactions, coats protein with uniform negative charge. (3) β-mercaptoethanol/DTT: reduces DISULFIDE BONDS specifically (these are covalent, NOT disrupted by urea alone). PHYSICAL: Heat (disrupts H-bonds, hydrophobic interactions), extreme pH (disrupts ionic interactions/H-bonds), mechanical agitation. Denaturation: loss of biological activity, increased viscosity, decreased solubility, exposure of hydrophobic residues. REVERSIBLE: some proteins (e.g., ribonuclease — Anfinsen experiment) can REFOLD when denaturant removed → proves primary sequence contains all information for 3D structure (Anfinsen's dogma). Reference: Harper's — Protein Denaturation.

questions.questionText: A mutational defect affecting the synthesis of desmosine and isodesmosine cross-links would most likely result in impaired function of which of the following tissues?
questions.optionB: Aortic wall
questions.explanation: Desmosine and isodesmosine are specialized cross-linking amino acids unique to elastin. They are formed from condensation of multiple allysine residues (produced by lysyl oxidase). Elastin is a major component of the aorta and large arteries (allowing for stretch and recoil). Disruption leads to aortic pathology.
